old_title: Iran war latest: Houthis strike Saudi oil tankers as Tehran warns of retaliation
new_title: Iran war latest: Marco Rubio claims Tehran is 'begging for a deal'
old_text:
Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi rebels have bombed two Saudi oil tankers off the coast of Saudi Arabia, threatening to further disrupt oil supply in a sign that the war is spreading across the region.
The Houthis claimed an attack on two vessels 70 nautical miles southwest of the port of Al Shuqaiq, saying they had violated a maritime blockade.
The incident had earlier been reported by the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations (UKMTO), which said crews were attempting to extinguish a fire onboard. No casualties or environmental impact have been reported.
According to the Saudi Press Agency (SPA), which cited an official from the General Transport Authority, the incident caused a fire at the front of the ship.
It came after Iran's foreign minister warned of an "eye for an eye" response if Donald Trump continues with his threat to bomb a bridge or power plant for every ship targeted in the Strait of Hormuz.
The US military said it had carried out a 12th straight night of strikes against Iran on Thursday.
Major security gathering in Southeast Asia focusses on Middle East
Top diplomats from Asia, Europe and North America met at the security-focused ASEAN Regional Forum, where ministers were expected to call for a peaceful settlement of renewed and intensifying hostilities in the Middle East.
The war in Iran and the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz are set to dominate the Manila meetings attended by US secretary of state Marco Rubio, Russian foreign minister Sergei Lavrov and top officials from Australia, Britain, China, the European Union, India, Japan and others.
The conflict is a key concern for the 11-nation Association of Southeast Asian Nations, which has a combined gross domestic product of about $3.8 trillion (£2.8 trillion) heavily reliant on Middle East oil, exposing it to fallout that has stoked global inflation.
"We convene against the backdrop of profound geopolitical and geoeconomic uncertainty," Maria Theresa Lazaro, the foreign secretary of the Philippines, which is chairing the grouping, said in opening remarks.
Iran's Revolutionary Guard claims it carried out attacks on US assets in Jordan
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) claimed it carried out attacks on US military assets in Jordan, saying it "destroyed a radar of the American THAAD missile defence system with lightning strikes".
It said a "Patriot system and a C-RAM radar were also targeted and destroyed, and the fuel tanks of the American base were set on fire".
It added that "a large helicopter equipment warehouse and a helicopter repair and maintenance shed were also set on fire and destroyed".
The IRGC said the strikes were aimed at US forces, not Jordan, and argued they were a response to American attacks on Iran.
It claimed the operations did not violate Jordan's sovereignty.
Jordan had not immediately commented on the claims.
The IRGC had earlier also claimed attacks on US military targets in Kuwait.
US uses B-1 long-range bomber for first time since fighting against Iran resumed
The US has escalated its military campaign against Iran by using a B-1 long-range bomber for the first time since fighting resumed 12 days ago, according to US officials.
The bomber was used to strike IRGC targets in Iran, Axios reported. B-1 aircraft can carry large numbers of heavy bombs and cruise missiles.
US Centcom said its strikes targeted Iranian military operations centres, maritime capabilities, aircraft hangars, drone storage facilities and logistics infrastructure to weaken Tehran's ability to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
However, it remains unclear what specific targets the B-1 hit.
Regional mediators push for new 10-day ceasefire between US and Iran
Qatar, Pakistan and Egypt have proposed a 10-day ceasefire between the US and Iran in an effort to prevent the conflict from spreading into a wider regional war, Axios reported.
The proposal comes after continuous US strikes on Iran and continued Iranian attacks targeting Gulf states, including Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan.
Iran has vowed an "eye for an eye" response after US president Donald Trump threatened strikes on Iranian infrastructure for attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz.
Iran war: Key developments today
- US strikes Iran for the 12th consecutive night.
- Iran says at least two people were killed and 11 wounded in the US strike on the Shalamcheh border crossing with Iraq.
- The IRGC said it hit US military facilities in Kuwait, including drone and air defence sites.
- Tehran said it stopped three oil tankers from crossing the Strait of Hormuz, with one vessel catching fire. The IRGC said the waterway was "completely closed".
- Yemen's Houthis claimed attacks on two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea. Saudi Arabia confirmed the Encelia was hit but said all crew were safe.
- The US president warned Washington would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant for every attack on ships in the Strait of Hormuz.
- Iranian foreign minister Abbas Araghchi warned of an "eye for an eye" response to any attack on Iranian infrastructure.
- Oil prices climbed to around $95 a barrel amid fears of wider disruption to global energy supplies.
US carries out 12th straight night of strikes as Iran-backed Houthi rebels bomb two Saudi oil tankers
Iranian state media report two dead in latest US strikes
Iranian state media reported that two people were killed in US strikes in southern Iran, near the country's border with Iraq.
The deaths were reported as part of the latest wave of American attacks, which the US military said targeted "maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites, and air defence assets".
Centcom said in a statement on X: "The strikes further degrade Iran's ability to attack civilian mariners and commercial vessels."
According to Al Jazeera, explosions were reported in cities including Bushehr, Ahvaz, Sirik and Shalamcheh.
Centcom earlier today posted on X, saying it completed the 12th night of strikes on Iran.
Araghchi describes moment of US-Israeli strike that killed Ali Khamenei
Iran's foreign minister Abbas Araghchi has described surviving a US-Israeli strike that killed Ali Khamenei on the first day of the war, saying he was pulled from rubble after a leadership compound was hit.
In an interview with Iranian journalist Javad Mogouyi, Araghchi said the US and Israel had accurate intelligence on the location of Iran's top officials.
"What matters, in my view, is the fact that Israel had intelligence about these meetings," Araghchi said, adding: "This may point to a security gap that probably still exists. This needs to be fixed."
He said he has since been constantly moving around Tehran for security reasons.
Describing the strike, he said: "We were on the other side of the building, and after the explosion, the ceiling, walls, and everything collapsed on us. I grabbed Mr Hijazi's hand (Khamenei's deputy chief of staff, Ali Asghar Hijazi), and we made our way through the beams and boards and pieces of wood, hearing the cries for help."
He continued: "Water was pouring from the ceiling. Everything was wrecked. We eventually got out, and I saw that various parts of the compound had been targeted."
House passes defense bill with Trump's $1T spending request and Department of War rename
The US House of Representatives has approved a defense policy bill that includes President Donald Trump's request for a substantial $1.15 trillion in national security spending and proposes renaming the Department of Defense to the Department of War.
The legislation, which also mandates a 5 percent to 7 percent pay increase for service members next year based on rank, marks a significant departure from the typically bipartisan nature of the annual National Defense Authorization Act.
The vote, a narrow 216-212, underscored deep divisions, with Democrats voicing strong opposition. Their concerns centered on the considerable spending hike for the Pentagon, particularly as Republicans simultaneously sought to cut numerous non-defense programs through other legislative efforts.
Read on...
new_text:
US secretary of state Marco Rubio has claimed Iran is "begging for a deal" amid escalating tensions across the Middle East, while warning that Tehran will face intensifying US strikes "every night until they come to their senses".
Speaking at the ASEAN conference in Manila, Rubio said: "Iran is begging for a deal. It looks like Iran is not ready to make a deal, they will pay the price." He added: "Every time they make a deal they either break it or change it."
The US military said it had carried out a 12th straight night of strikes against Iran in the early hours of Thursday, including the first use of B-1 long-range bombers in weeks.
That came despite Iran's foreign minister warning of an "eye for an eye" response if Donald Trump continues with his threat to bomb a bridge or power plant for every ship targeted in the Strait of Hormuz.
Meanwhile, Yemen's Iran-backed Houthi rebels bombed two Saudi oil tankers off the coast of Saudi Arabia, threatening to further disrupt oil supply in a sign that the war is spreading across the region.
Iran could be the richest country in the Middle East, Rubio says
US secretary of state Marco Rubio has said that Iran's "lunatic" leaders, including the clerics, are holding the country back and that it could be "the richest country in the Middle East".
"Some of them are lunatics, some of them are evil, some of them are evil lunatics," he told reporters. He then added: "You can be both."
He said that Iran "is a country run by radical clerics," who are "oblivious" to economic issues.
"They are destroying their country," he said, adding: "But instead they take their money, and they use it and they give it to Hezbollah, they give it to Hamas, they give it to the Houthis. They give it to Shia militias. They give it to sponsored terrorism all over the world."
Rubio also repeated the US position that Iran can't have a nuclear weapon. "They cannot have nuclear weapons; I mean, imagine what they would do to the world," he said.
US uses B-1 long-range bomber for first time since fighting against Iran resumed
The US has escalated its military campaign against Iran by using a B-1 long-range bomber for the first time since fighting resumed 12 days ago, according to US officials.
The bomber was used to strike IRGC targets in Iran, Axios reported. B-1 aircraft can carry large numbers of heavy bombs and cruise missiles.
US Centcom said its strikes targeted Iranian military operations centres, maritime capabilities, aircraft hangars, drone storage facilities and logistics infrastructure to weaken Tehran's ability to threaten commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
However, it remains unclear what specific targets the B-1 hit.
Rubio claims Iran 'begging for a deal' amid escalating US strikes
The US secretary of state has claimed that Iran is "begging for a deal" with the US and that the "price on Iran will get higher every night until they come to their senses".
Speaking at the ASEAN conference in Manila, Marco Rubio said: "Iran is begging for a deal. It looks like Iran is not ready to make a deal, they will pay the price."
He added: "Every time they make a deal they either break it or change it."
Rubio said he hoped the Iran-backed Houthis will de-escalate. "They got snookered by Iran."
The secretary of state also commented on the nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia, saying: "Even when the official announcement is made, it will have to go through a congressional notification process.
"And then we can talk more in depth about it, but suffice it to say that the US is always looking for co-operation agreements with allies."
He called Saudi Arabia "a strategic ally" of Washington.
Iranian state media report two dead in latest US strikes
Iranian state media reported that two people were killed and 11 wounded in US strikes in southern Iran, near the country's border with Iraq.
The deaths were reported as part of the latest wave of American attacks, which the US military said targeted "maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites, and air defence assets".
Centcom said in a statement on X: "The strikes further degrade Iran's ability to attack civilian mariners and commercial vessels."
According to Al Jazeera, explosions were reported in cities including Bushehr, Ahvaz, Sirik and Shalamcheh.
Centcom earlier today posted on X, saying it completed the 12th night of strikes on Iran.
Iran's Revolutionary Guard defends its strikes on Gulf states
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) defended its attacks on US military bases in Kuwait and Jordan, arguing that the strikes were aimed at American forces rather than the Gulf states themselves.
Responding to accusations that Iran had violated the sovereignty of countries where US bases are located, the IRGC said such claims were "completely incorrect", according to Mehr state news agency.
In a message to Kuwait, it said: "It is America that has violated your territorial integrity and sovereignty, not us."
The IRGC argued that US military bases in Gulf countries were effectively under American control and claimed local officials could not access them without US permission.
It said: "We are attacking lands occupied by the US army."
The group added that the bases were being used to carry out "crimes" against Iran and said: "It is our legal, religious and logical right to target the invader of our country."
In a separate statement directed at Jordan, the IRGC said: "In our opinion, the independence, territorial integrity and sovereignty of your government are completely respected."
US carries out 12th straight night of strikes as Iran-backed Houthi rebels bomb two Saudi oil tankers
British government withdraws staff from Iran
The UK government has temporarily withdrawn embassy staff from Iran, citing "the ongoing security situation" in the region.
The British embassy in Tehran will continue operating remotely, according to a brief update on the government's website.
The statement from the Foreign Office said that it "advises against all travel to Iran. If you are a British national already in Iran, either resident or visitor, carefully consider your presence there and the risks you take by staying".
The move comes after days of consecutive nights of US strikes on Iran and amid escalating tensions following Donald Trump's threat to target Iranian infrastructure, including power plants and bridges in Tehran.
Israelis warn Saudi-US nuclear deal will lead to 'crazy arms race'
Senior Israeli politicians have warned a deal between the US and Saudi Arabia allowing the kingdom to build nuclear reactors using American technology and to enrich uranium could prompt the start of an arms race in the Middle East.
The agreement, which still needs Congressional approval but was announced on Wednesday, is meant to allow the Saudis to develop a civilian nuclear programme.
Netanyahu and his defence and foreign ministers have so far not commented on the deal, which critics say compromises Israel's long-term security interests.
Former prime minister Naftali Bennett, who is seeking to unseat Netanyahu in an October 27 election, said: "The nuclear agreement that is coming together with Saudi Arabia, over Israel's head, is a serious strategic failure that endangers our security."
"Nuclear enrichment on Saudi soil could lead to a regional nuclear race and a dangerous loss of control."
Former defence minister Avigdor Lieberman said that "the civilian nuclear program in Saudi Arabia will end in nuclear weapons and will lead to a crazy arms race throughout the entire Middle East."
Major security gathering in Southeast Asia focusses on Middle East
Top diplomats from Asia, Europe and North America met at the security-focused ASEAN Regional Forum, where ministers were expected to call for a peaceful settlement of renewed and intensifying hostilities in the Middle East.
The war in Iran and the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz are set to dominate the Manila meetings attended by US secretary of state Marco Rubio, Russian foreign minister Sergei Lavrov and top officials from Australia, Britain, China, the European Union, India, Japan and others.
The conflict is a key concern for the 11-nation Association of Southeast Asian Nations, which has a combined gross domestic product of about $3.8 trillion (£2.8 trillion) heavily reliant on Middle East oil, exposing it to fallout that has stoked global inflation.
"We convene against the backdrop of profound geopolitical and geoeconomic uncertainty," Maria Theresa Lazaro, the foreign secretary of the Philippines, which is chairing the grouping, said in opening remarks.
Iran's Revolutionary Guard claims it carried out attacks on US assets in Jordan
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) claimed it carried out attacks on US military assets in Jordan, saying it "destroyed a radar of the American THAAD missile defence system with lightning strikes".
It said a "Patriot system and a C-RAM radar were also targeted and destroyed, and the fuel tanks of the American base were set on fire".
It added that "a large helicopter equipment warehouse and a helicopter repair and maintenance shed were also set on fire and destroyed".
The IRGC said the strikes were aimed at US forces, not Jordan, and argued they were a response to American attacks on Iran.
It claimed the operations did not violate Jordan's sovereignty.
Jordan had not immediately commented on the claims.
The IRGC had earlier also claimed attacks on US military targets in Kuwait.
